title: Hello, World
lesson:
Real MIPS programs (on the N64 or anywhere else) talk to the outside world through 'syscall', the same idea as the PDP-8's IOT instruction wrapped up as a service number. We're using the MARS/SPIM convention students learn in architecture courses: put a service code in $v0, an argument in $a0, and call syscall.

The services we'll use:
  $v0 = 1  print integer in $a0
  $v0 = 4  print zero-terminated string at address in $a0
  $v0 = 10 exit

.asciiz lays a string into memory followed by a zero byte. 'la $a0, msg' points $a0 at the start, syscall 4 prints until it hits the zero. Same pattern as the LC-3's PUTS, just spelled differently.
prompt: Print HELLO using syscall 4. Put the string in a .asciiz, load its address into $a0, set $v0 = 4, syscall, then exit with $v0 = 10.
starterCode:
.data
msg:    .asciiz "HELLO"
.text
        la   $a0, msg
        li   $v0, 4            # syscall 4 = print string at $a0
        syscall
        li   $v0, 10
        syscall
hints: la $a0, msg loads the address of the string into $a0.
li $v0, 4 then syscall prints the zero-terminated string at $a0.
Finish with li $v0, 10 and syscall to exit cleanly.
reference:
.data
msg: .asciiz "HELLO"
.text
la $a0, msg
li $v0, 4
syscall
li $v0, 10
syscall
